VO_EN: Your son counts to ninety before he falls asleep.
You're a UX designer at a startup in Tel Aviv.
You design buttons for a delivery app.
The kind that tells a driver where to turn left.
The driver checks the shelter map before every route.
You just design the buttons inside it.
You step on LEGO at 2 AM and swear quietly.
The siren doesn't ask if you're ready.
What you do with them is the rest of your life.
Before the escalation Tel Aviv is noise and heat and four hundred sixty thousand people complaining about rent.
Rothschild Boulevard on a Friday afternoon.
Students drink iced coffee on Ibn Gabirol.
Couples walk the promenade at sunset.
The Mediterranean doesn't care about borders.
The salt air sticks to your phone screen.
You wipe it with your shirt.
Every morning you take the train from Holon to HaShalom.
Your coworker is angry because Maccabi lost.
He's been angry about it since Sunday.
You argue about roster changes on the platform.
The barista at your corner cafe draws a smiley face on your cup.
You bring rugelach home from Dizengoff on Fridays.
Your wife says you buy too many.
Your son leaves LEGO on the floor.
You step on it in the dark every single time.
You've learned to keep living between them.
Your son was born into this.
You don't remember when you stopped flinching.
You will not understand what this city was until it stops being this.
It starts not with a siren but with frequency.
Your phone buzzes so often your son says Daddy's phone is singing again.
By Thursday your thumb swipes the alert before your brain reads it.
You don't remember when that became automatic.
The app you designed goes quiet.
The buttons you designed are still there.
The fact that your nervous system can't reset between them.
Your body is up before your brain.
The floor is cold under your bare feet.
You don't feel them until the hallway.
Your son is heavy and warm and still half-asleep on your shoulder.
Your hand slips on the metal.
You feel a sting but you don't look.
His LEGO set is already in here.
You moved it three days ago without thinking about why.
Your heart is in your throat.
You wonder if your son can hear it too.
The pressure drops in your ears.
That pop that means someone above you did their job.
Sulfur seeps through the window seal.
The smell hits your nose before the all-clear.
Your son is already asleep again.
The Iron Dome catches ninety percent.
Your son watches the interceptions from the balcony.
White streaks crossing the sky like angry fireflies.
In a barrage of two hundred that's twenty impacts somewhere.
You don't know where until you hear it.
When an interception flashes your ears pop.
You're watching your survival calculated above your head.
It's math that waits for the wrong night.
By day seven your body runs the countdown without you.
You've shaved two seconds off since the first night.
Your feet know the path in the dark.
Your hands find the handle without looking.
Your son sleeps through the pickup now.
He doesn't wake up when you lift him.
He doesn't wake up when you run.
His body has adapted to being carried to safety in the dark.
That's what resilience looks like from outside.
From inside it's the moment you realize your son's nervous system has been rewritten.
That detail destroys you more than any explosion.
Ninety seconds feels like nothing now.
Your neighbor gets the call on Tuesday.
Kisses his daughter at the door.
His wife stands in the hallway after he's gone.
Her jaw is set but her hands are shaking.
You bring them dinner that night.
You don't know what to say so you don't say anything.
His daughter plays LEGO with your son in the mamad during the next siren.
Your son builds a dome over it.
Two weeks later she stops answering messages.
On the news they announce casualties without names.
You sit on the couch with the volume low.
You count the names that aren't his.
That is how you hope now.
Your sister in New York calls.
People at her office are arguing about the war.
She doesn't know what to say when they ask her to condemn.
She asks you what to say.
You're standing in the mamad at 2 AM.
Your son is asleep on your chest.
His breathing is warm on your neck.
You say I don't know what to say either.
The world has an opinion about your country.
You don't have a country right now.
You have a mamad and ninety seconds and a boy who weighs twenty-two kilos on your chest.
You're a father in a concrete room.
The concrete is the only thing between his sleep and the sky.
Impact is a sound that moves through your skeleton.
A car alarm starts screaming outside.
Eight hundred meters from your son's school.
The school's been closed for a week.
But you drive past the crater on the way to the supermarket.
A swing set on its side.
A backpack hanging on the fence.
Your son sees it from the car window.
That night he walks into the mamad without being told.
You wait for him to start.
Rubble that used to be someone's mamad.
Children who look like your son.
You're relieved it's not your building.
Your wife says turn it off.
You don't know what you're looking for.
Grief for people you don't know.
Nobody in your building talks about it.
You feel it in the elevator.
After ten days you go back to the cafe.
The barista is wearing the same apron.
Something behind her eyes has shifted.
Your hand wraps around the warm cup.
The only warmth that feels normal.
After the all-clear everyone walks back.
You drink it like nothing happened.
Because if you don't you'll put the cup down and scream.
Your son asks: are the booms the good ones or the bad ones.
There are good booms and bad booms.
He learned the difference in two weeks.
He places a tiny figure inside.
The figure is safe under the dome.
The dome is made of plastic.
You press your forehead against the mirror.
Your hands grip the sink edge.
The porcelain is cold under your fingers.
When the ceasefire comes nothing returns.
Your son won't go on the swings.
The cafe is open but the barista moved to Berlin.
A new girl makes your coffee.
No smiley face on the cup.
Your neighbor came back from miluim.
He doesn't talk about where he was.
He smokes on the balcony at 3 AM.
You see his cigarette glow from yours.
Your wife sleeps with her hand on the go-bag.
The LEGO dome is on the shelf in the mamad.
The tiny figure is still inside.
Your son still counts to ninety before he sleeps.
That is how you know something broke.
A car door slams on the street.
Your feet move toward the hallway before your brain says stop.
You stand in the hallway for a moment.
Everyone says that should be enough.
